title: Module 1: Simple Reflections - Building Engagement
learning_objective: Learn to use simple reflections to demonstrate understanding and build rapport with a patient in precontemplation
scenario_context: Simple reflections repeat or rephrase what the patient said to show you're listening and understand. They build trust and encourage the patient to continue exploring.

persona_config.name: Yeah,
persona_config.background: Does not recognize the problem or feels it's not significant enough to address.

node_1.persona_text: My doctor sent me here to talk about quitting smoking, but honestly, I don't see what the big deal is. I've been smoking for 20 years and I'm fine.

choice_points.option_text: Your doctor is concerned about your smoking.

feedback.immediate: This reflects only part of what the patient said - you missed their perspective that they don't see it as a problem. A more complete reflection would acknowledge their view.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: You don't see your smoking as a problem right now.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This simple reflection captures the patient's perspective without judgment. It shows you heard them and creates space for them to continue.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: Even though you've been smoking for 20 years, it's actually causing damage you can't see yet.

feedback.immediate: This contradicts the patient and jumps to educating them. The 'righting reflex' often increases resistance in precontemplation. Try reflecting their perspective first.
feedback.learning_note: Watch for patient resistance signals - defensiveness or disengagement.

node_2a.persona_text: Yeah, she is. But she worries about everything. I feel fine, my breathing is okay. I don't get what everyone's so worried about.

choice_points.option_text: From your perspective, everything feels fine with your health.

feedback.immediate: Good reflection! You're continuing to show understanding of their perspective, which builds rapport even when they're resistant.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: Your breathing feels okay to you right now.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This reflects a specific detail the patient mentioned, showing you're listening carefully. Specificity strengthens the reflection.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: But are you sure your breathing is really okay? Sometimes people don't notice gradual changes.

feedback.immediate: This challenges the patient's perception and can increase defensiveness. In precontemplation, reflecting their view builds more rapport than questioning it.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_2b.persona_text: Exactly! I mean, if I had a problem, I'd know it, right? I can still do everything I used to do.

choice_points.option_text: You feel like you'd notice if smoking was affecting you.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This reflection captures their reasoning and continues to build understanding. You're resisting the urge to correct or educate.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: You can still do everything you used to do.

feedback.immediate: Good! This is a very literal reflection. While accurate, the previous option added slightly more depth by capturing the underlying belief.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: Actually, many smokers don't realize they're being affected until serious damage has occurred.

feedback.immediate: This is the 'righting reflex' - jumping to educate. While factually correct, it breaks rapport and increases resistance. Stay with reflections in early engagement.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_2c.persona_text: Look, I appreciate you trying to help, but I didn't come here to be lectured. I get enough of that from my family.

choice_points.option_text: You're feeling lectured, and that's frustrating for you.

feedback.immediate: Excellent recovery! Reflecting their emotion (frustration) shows empathy and can help repair the rapport. This is 'rolling with resistance.'
feedback.learning_note: Watch for patient resistance signals - defensiveness or disengagement.

choice_points.option_text: I'm not here to lecture you. I genuinely want to help you be healthier.

feedback.immediate: Defending yourself can make things worse. Instead, reflect the patient's experience to show you understand their frustration.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_3a.persona_text: Right. I mean, sure, I cough in the morning sometimes, but that's normal for smokers, isn't it?

choice_points.option_text: You notice some coughing, and you see that as just part of being a smoker.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This reflects both parts - the symptom and their interpretation - without judgment. You're building a complete picture of their perspective.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: You cough in the mornings.

feedback.immediate: This is accurate but misses the patient's interpretation (that they see it as normal). The more complete reflection captures their full meaning.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: That morning cough is actually called 'smoker's cough' and it means your lungs are trying to clear out tar and toxins.

feedback.immediate: While educational, this breaks the engagement process. You're still building rapport - save education for later when they're more open to it.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_3b.persona_text: You know what, I don't think this is for me. Thanks anyway.

node_3c.persona_text: Yeah. I walk my dog every day, I work a physical job. If smoking was really hurting me, I couldn't do those things.

choice_points.option_text: Your daily activities - walking your dog, your physical job - those are evidence to you that you're doing okay.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This reflection amplifies what they said, showing you really understand their reasoning. This builds strong rapport.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: You work a physical job.

feedback.immediate: Accurate but incomplete. You captured one detail but missed the connection they're making between their activities and their health assessment.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_3d.persona_text: Yeah, it is. Everyone acts like they know what's best for me better than I do.

choice_points.option_text: You feel like people aren't respecting that you know yourself best.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This reflection captures the deeper meaning - their need for autonomy and respect. This kind of reflection deepens rapport.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: People are telling you what to do.

feedback.immediate: Accurate but stays at the surface. The previous option captures the emotional impact - feeling disrespected - which shows deeper empathy.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_4a.persona_text: Exactly. My dad smoked till he was 75 and lived a good life. I figure genetics matter more than people think.

choice_points.option_text: Your dad's experience - smoking and still living to 75 - that shapes how you think about risk.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This reflection shows you understand where their beliefs come from. You've built solid rapport through consistent reflection.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: You think genetics are more important than smoking.

feedback.immediate: Good reflection. Both options are MI-adherent; the first adds slightly more context about why they believe this.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_4b.persona_text: Right. So when my doctor gets all serious about it, it's hard to take seriously, you know?

choice_points.option_text: There's a gap between what your doctor is saying and what you're experiencing in your body.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This reflection identifies the discrepancy they're experiencing. You've demonstrated deep understanding throughout this conversation.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: It's hard to take your doctor seriously about this.

feedback.immediate: Good reflection! Both options work well here - the first adds a bit more depth about why it's hard to take seriously.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_4c.persona_text: Thanks for understanding that. I guess I'm just tired of feeling judged, you know?

choice_points.option_text: You're tired of feeling judged about your smoking.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! By consistently reflecting without judgment, you've repaired the relationship and created an opening for future conversations.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_end_good.persona_text: Yeah. Look, I appreciate you actually listening instead of just telling me what to do. Maybe we can talk again sometime.

node_end_resistance.persona_text: Right. Well, I think I'm done here. Have a good day.
